real.title: Sink HTTP/ClickHouse clients follow redirects by default; a redirected POST ending in 2xx is acked as committed though the body was never delivered (false ack)
real.claim: Both sink HTTP clients are built with the reqwest default redirect policy: HttpSink (http.rs:113-124) and ClickHouseSink (clickhouse.rs:211-219) call Client::builder() and never .redirect(...). reqwest 0.12's Policy::default() is Policy::limited(10) (verified in ~/.cargo reqwest-0.12 src/redirect.rs:160-163), so up to 10 redirects are followed automatically; grep confirms no redirect policy anywhere under crates/weir-server/src/sink/. In HttpSink::post_record, any status.is_success() returns Ok(()) (http.rs:157-164), and the record is pushed to `committed` in commit() (http.rs:339). When the configured endpoint (or a fronting proxy/LB) answers the POST with 301/302/303, reqwest re-issues the request as a BODILESS GET to the Location target; if that returns 2xx the sink observes success and marks the record committed even though the payload bytes were never delivered to a real handler. The drain then confirms+deletes the segment on BatchResult::Ok (drain/mod.rs), so the record is gone: a false ack, violating the crown invariant. ClickHouse::commit has the identical exposure — a redirected POST ending in 2xx returns `committed: batch` (clickhouse.rs:350-355) although the INSERT ... FORMAT RowBinary may never have executed. Corroborating evidence that 3xx was assumed observable: health() has a now-dead `|| resp.status().is_redirection()` arm (http.rs:381) that reqwest can never reach because it follows the redirect before returning; and neither commit path classifies 3xx at all (classify_status_transient only matches 408/429/5xx at http.rs:244-251; status_is_transient likewise at clickhouse.rs:242-246; the module doc http.rs:20-27 enumerates 2xx/4xx/transient but is silent on 3xx). 307/308 preserve the body but can redirect to a host the operator never configured (misconfigured or attacker-controlled Location), committing to the wrong destination.
real.suggested_fix: Set .redirect(reqwest::redirect::Policy::none()) on both sink clients (http.rs:113, clickhouse.rs:211) and explicitly classify 3xx on the commit path as permanent (dead-letter) or transient (retry) — never committed. Disabling redirects also makes the existing health() is_redirection() arm reachable and correct.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at the cited locations. Neither sink sets a redirect policy: HttpSink (http.rs:113-124) and ClickHouseSink (clickhouse.rs:211-219) call Client::builder() with no .redirect(...). reqwest 0.12.28 default is redirect::Policy::default() (client.rs:310) == Policy::limited(10) (redirect.rs:161-164), so up to 10 redirects are followed. tower_http follow_redirect/mod.rs:261-274 confirms a POST hitting 301/302 (MOVED_PERMANENTLY/FOUND) or 303 (SEE_OTHER) is rewritten to Method::GET with BodyRepr::Empty — i.e. a bodiless GET; the payload bytes are never delivered. If that GET returns 2xx, HttpSink::post_record's status.is_success() returns Ok (http.rs:158-164) and the record is pushed to committed (http.rs:339); ClickHouseSink::commit returns committed: batch (clickhouse.rs:350-355). The drain then accumulates durable_through on BatchResult::Ok (drain/mod.rs:663) and confirm_and_deletes the segment (drain/mod.rs:496-497) — the record is gone after an ack though the body was never delivered: a false ack. 3xx is unclassified in both commit paths: classify_status_transient matches only 408/429/5xx (http.rs:244-251), status_is_transient likewise (clickhouse.rs:242-246), and the module doc (http.rs:18-30) enumerates only 2xx/4xx/transient. The health() is_redirection() arm (http.rs:381) is indeed unreachable given redirect-following, corroborating the assumption. No prior FINDINGS.md entry mentions redirect/3xx/301-308.

real.title: Transient scan_and_advance_counter() failure leaves next_counter at 1, re-opening the F12 segment-overwrite data-loss hole via a new trigger
real.suggested_fix: Do not treat a scan_and_advance_counter() failure as benign. Either (a) propagate the io::Error out of flusher_thread and take the shard permanently offline (Nack-everything, the same end state as the respawn-cap path at mod.rs:166) so nothing is ever sealed at an unestablished counter, or (b) before opening/sealing the first segment, refuse to proceed if any seg_*.wab.sealed exists in the shard dir while the counter could not be established. A cheaper local defense: have ensure_open()/seal() refuse to seal when the target .wab.sealed already exists (turn the implicit overwrite into an explicit error). Add a regression test that injects a segment_counters() read_dir error with a pre-existing seg_00000001.wab.sealed and asserts the recovered sealed segment is never overwritten (the existing scan_and_advance_counter_advances_past_sealed_segments test only covers the happy path with .unwrap()).
real.claim: flusher_thread() calls writer.scan_and_advance_counter() (mod.rs:439) and on ANY error merely logs `failed to scan segment counters; starting at 1` (mod.rs:440) and proceeds. scan_and_advance_counter (segment.rs:435-446) computes max from store.segment_counters(...)? — the `?` short-circuits BEFORE next_counter is touched, so on error next_counter stays at its constructor default of 1 (new_with_store, segment.rs:424). The scan's only fallible step is std::fs::read_dir(dir)? inside FsSegmentStore::segment_counters (segment.rs:358), which fails transiently under fd pressure (EMFILE/ENFILE), backing-fs EIO, or ENOMEM. Crucially, recover_open_segments() runs earlier in spawn() on the calling thread (mod.rs:231) and recover_segment() renames every crashed active .wab to .wab.sealed (recovery.rs:329), so at flusher start a shard dir can legitimately hold ONLY recovered-but-undrained seg_NNNNNNNN.wab.sealed files. If the counter scan then fails, ensure_open() (segment.rs:499-506) opens its first segment as seg_00000001.wab — WabSegment::create uses create_new(true)/O_EXCL (segment.rs:59) which enforces non-existence of the .wab name only and does NOT collide with an existing .wab.sealed — and that segment's first seal does std::fs::rename(seg_00000001.wab -> seg_00000001.wab.sealed) (segment.rs:258), atomically REPLACING the recovered, undrained sealed segment. Those records were acked durable to producers but are lost before the drain delivered them: the exact crown-invariant violation F12 (commit fb02a62) was meant to eliminate. The F12 fix only corrected segment_counter_from_path parsing (file_stem -> leading-digit-run, segment.rs:294-304) so sealed files are counted on the success path; it never touched the mod.rs:439-441 error branch, so the same data-loss outcome stays reachable via this distinct (transient read_dir) trigger. The DST SimSegmentStore::segment_counters delegates straight to FsSegmentStore (dst.rs:427-429) and injects no fault on the scan, so DST does not cover it, and the regression test scan_and_advance_counter_advances_past_sealed_segments (segment.rs:756) asserts only the happy path with .unwrap().
real.verdict_reason: Every link in the chain is verified in the current code. mod.rs:439-441 logs and proceeds on any scan_and_advance_counter() error. segment.rs:435-446: the `?` on segment_counters (line 438) short-circuits before next_counter is assigned, leaving it at the constructor default 1 (segment.rs:424). The scan's only fallible step is std::fs::read_dir(dir)? (segment.rs:358), transiently fallible under EMFILE/ENFILE/ENOMEM/EIO. recover_open_segments runs earlier in spawn() (mod.rs:231) and recovery.rs:328-329 renames crashed active .wab to .wab.sealed, so a shard dir can legitimately hold ONLY recovered-but-undrained seg_NNNNNNNN.wab.sealed at flusher start. ensure_open then opens seg_00000001.wab via create_new(true)+O_NOFOLLOW (segment.rs:59), which enforces non-existence of the .wab name only and does NOT collide with the existing .wab.sealed; the first seal() does std::fs::rename(.wab -> .wab.sealed) (segment.rs:258), atomically replacing the recovered undrained sealed segment — the exact F12 crown-invariant data-loss outcome. The F12 fix (fb02a62) diff touches only segment.rs (counter parsing on the success path) and never the mod.rs error branch, so this distinct transient trigger remains reachable. DST cannot cover it: SimSegmentStore::segment_counters (dst.rs:427-429) delegates to FsSegmentStore with no fault injection. The regression test scan_and_advance_counter_advances_past_sealed_segments (segment.rs:756) uses .unwrap() at line 783 and exercises only the happy path.

real.title: Opt-in read timeout can desync the connection so a stale response is read as the next push's ack (false ack)
real.claim: read_response() (unix.rs:154-189) reads each response in three separate read_exact calls (header at :156, payload at :175, CRC at :179). With the F43 opt-in read timeout set (set_read_timeout, unix.rs:250), a timeout that fires mid-frame returns Err but the bytes already drained from the socket are gone. WeirClient (unix.rs:81-86) holds NO poisoned/closed/desynced flag — only `stream` and `default_durability` — so after the `?`-propagated error the same client is fully reusable. The set_read_timeout doc (unix.rs:244-249) explicitly tells the producer to 'retry' on the Io timeout ('the record may still have been durably written') without warning that the connection MUST be discarded. If the producer retries push() on the SAME WeirClient, the leftover bytes of slow response N are consumed as the response to push N+1: if response N was an Ack but N+1 was actually Nacked (or never processed), push N+1 returns Ok(()) — a FALSE ACK, directly violating the crown invariant. This hazard is NEW: F43 added the timeout, F44 added only the alloc cap; neither addresses reuse-after-timeout, and the only timeout test (read_timeout_bounds_a_silent_daemon_instead_of_blocking_forever, unix.rs:376) does a single push and never reuses the client.
real.suggested_fix: On any Io error in read_response (especially a timeout), mark the WeirClient poisoned so subsequent push/health_check fail fast, or document loudly that the connection MUST be dropped and re-established after a timeout. Add a test that a second push on a timed-out client does not read a stale frame.
real.verdict_reason: The cited facts all hold. unix.rs:154-189 reads each response in three separate read_exact calls (header :156, payload :175, CRC :179); std's default_read_exact (confirmed in rustc 1.95 source) advances its buffer as bytes arrive and on a propagated Err (the SO_RCVTIMEO TimedOut/WouldBlock from set_read_timeout, unix.rs:250) returns without restoring already-consumed bytes — so a timeout firing mid-frame irrecoverably drains partial bytes. WeirClient (unix.rs:81-86) carries only `stream` + `default_durability`, no poisoned/desynced flag, so the same client is fully reusable after the ?-propagated error, and the only retry guidance (unix.rs:244-249) tells the producer to retry without saying the connection must be discarded. lib.rs and tls.rs add no such warning. This is genuinely NEW: FINDINGS.md/ESCALATIONS.md show F43 added the timeout and F44 added only the alloc cap; neither addressed reuse-after-timeout. So the underlying defect (indeterminate framing state after an I/O error + no poison flag + retry-inviting doc) is real and not already known. HOWEVER the finder over-escalates the consequence: a silent false Ack requires the misaligned residual bytes to decode as a structurally valid Ack — but Header::decode (envelope.rs:166-185) verifies WEIR magic, version, AND a 32-bit header CRC before message_type is consulted, and read_response then verifies a 32-bit payload CRC (unix.rs:182). Misframed mid-stream bytes overwhelmingly fail magic/CRC and surface as ClientError::Protocol, a hard error — not a silent Ok(()). A silent false Ack would need two independent CRC32 checks plus magic/version to collide by chance: astronomically improbable, not a practical crown-invariant breach. Also, the common silent-daemon timeout fires at byte 0 with nothing consumed, so no desync occurs there at all. The defensible bug is a connection-state/documentation correctness gap, not a false-ack hazard.

real.title: weir-ctl `dl drop --yes` bails on the first delete failure, leaving an unreported partial irreversible deletion
real.claim: cmd_dl_drop iterates segments and `std::fs::remove_file(p).map_err(...)?` (main.rs:358-360) returns on the FIRST failure. If file #k of N fails (EACCES, or the daemon raced and renamed/removed it), files #1..#k-1 are already irreversibly deleted but the only output is `weir-ctl: remove <path>: <err>` (via main.rs:122); the `dropped N segment(s)` summary (main.rs:361-365) never prints, so the operator cannot tell how many irreversible deletions actually happened, and a naive re-run would drop again. For an explicitly-irreversible command, partial progress should be reported.
real.suggested_fix: Accumulate per-file outcomes: count successes, collect failures, and print 'dropped X of N (Y failed: ...)' rather than bailing on the first error; exit non-zero if any failed.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at crates/weir-ctl/src/main.rs:358-359: the drop loop `for (p, _) in &segs { std::fs::remove_file(p).map_err(...)?; }` returns on the FIRST failure via `?`. dl_segments (lines 297-316) returns a sorted multi-element Vec, so the >1-file case is real. On the error path, main() (line 119-125) prints only `weir-ctl: remove <path>: <err>` and returns ExitCode::FAILURE; the `dropped N segment(s)` summary (lines 361-365) never executes. Thus if file #k fails, files #1..#k-1 are already irreversibly deleted but the operator gets no count of how many actually went — exactly as claimed. The claim's file:line citations all check out.

real.title: weir-ctl `segments` counts every dead-letter file while `dl list`/`dl drop` apply a `dl_*` suffix filter — the two views can disagree
real.claim: cmd_segments accounts the dead-letter directory by counting EVERY regular file (main.rs:190: `if f.path().is_file()` with no name filter), whereas dl_segments (used by `dl list`/`dl drop`) only matches files that start with `dl_` and end in `.wab.sealed`/`.wab` (main.rs:306-308). So `weir-ctl segments` and `weir-ctl dl list` can report a different dead-letter file count/byte total for the same directory — e.g. a stray `.tmp` partial, an operator note, or any non-`dl_` file inflates the `segments` view but not the `dl list` view. For a 1.0 operator tool, two surfaces describing the same store should agree.
real.suggested_fix: Reuse dl_segments() (or its predicate) inside cmd_segments' dead_letter branch so both commands apply the identical dl_* / suffix filter.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at the cited lines. cmd_segments (main.rs:190) counts every regular file in the dead_letter dir with `if f.path().is_file()` and no name filter, while dl_segments (main.rs:306-308, used by `dl list`/`dl drop`) requires `starts_with("dl_") && (ends_with(".wab.sealed") || ends_with(".wab"))`. Any foreign file (operator note, editor swap file, a `.confirmed` marker, etc.) is counted by `segments` but excluded by `dl list`, so the two operator surfaces can report different file/byte totals for the same directory. The finding's `.tmp`/partial example is slightly off — the dead-letter writer's own `dl_*.wab` partials ARE matched by dl_segments per its doc comment — but the divergence on genuinely-foreign files is real. Pure read-only observability discrepancy: no ack, no data path, CROWN INVARIANT untouched.

real.title: Client mTLS connector (connect_tls / PEM loaders / TLS timeout setters) has zero test coverage
real.claim: tls.rs (connect_tls at :37, load_certs at :93, load_key at :100, set_read_timeout/set_write_timeout at :80/:86) has no test: tls.rs has no #[cfg(test)] module, and no file under crates/weir-client/tests references connect_tls/load_key/load_certs/ClientTlsConfig (only tls.rs itself, the lib.rs module decl, and examples/push_tls.rs). The server side has TLS handshake tests, but the client mTLS path — cert/key loading error mapping, ServerName parsing, the aws-lc-rs provider/rustls builder chain — is entirely unexercised. For a 1.0 freeze of a security-relevant transport this is a meaningful gap; a regression in PEM parsing or the rustls builder chain would ship undetected. Parallels the test-coverage findings already accepted in the sweep (F11/F37/F38/F39/F51).
real.suggested_fix: Add a loopback round-trip integration test with generated test certs (client connects to a minimal rustls server over a TcpListener) plus unit tests for load_key/load_certs error mapping (missing key, malformed PEM).
real.verdict_reason: All cited locations verified against current crates/weir-client/src/tls.rs: connect_tls at :37, set_read_timeout/set_write_timeout at :80/:86, load_certs at :93, load_key at :100. tls.rs has no #[cfg(test)] module (only client #[cfg(test)] is unix.rs:282). No test references connect_tls/load_key/load_certs/ClientTlsConfig — the sole tests/ hit (proptest_client.rs:31) is set_read_timeout on the Unix client, not the TLS struct. Crucially, the server's tls_listener.rs DOES NOT use weir-client's connector: it builds an independent raw rustls client (its own local load_certs/load_key at :33/:40, its own ClientConfig chain at :26-94), so the public client mTLS path (PEM error mapping, ServerName parsing, aws-lc-rs/rustls builder chain) is genuinely never exercised. The set_read/write_timeout setters were added this session in commit 0eaa4d5 (within 65a39fb..HEAD, not-yet-swept) and have no test. This is a real, accurately-cited coverage gap on a security-relevant transport.

real.title: Dead-code allow on sink_url/sink_timeout_secs/sink_max_batch_size omits clickhouse-sink, contradicting the "non-noop sink arms" comment and require_sink_url's own four-feature gate
real.claim: The three `#[cfg_attr(not(any(feature="http-sink",feature="mysql-sink",feature="postgres-sink")), allow(dead_code))]` attributes on `sink_url` (mod.rs:317), `sink_timeout_secs` (mod.rs:326) and `sink_max_batch_size` (mod.rs:335) list only three sink features and omit `clickhouse-sink`, even though the ClickHouse arm in main.rs reads all three: `require_sink_url(&config, "clickhouse")` at main.rs:398 reads `sink_url`, and `config.sink_max_batch_size` / `config.sink_timeout_secs` are read at main.rs:413-414. The leading comment at mod.rs:306 calls these "only read by the non-noop sink arms" — but ClickHouse IS a non-noop arm and is excluded. This is inconsistent with the sibling helper `require_sink_url`, whose own `#[cfg(any(...))]` at main.rs:134-139 correctly lists all four features including `feature="clickhouse-sink"`. It is currently harmless (on a clickhouse-only build `not(any(...))` is true so `allow(dead_code)` is applied, but the fields are actually live there, making the allow a redundant no-op, so it does not break `-D warnings`), but the gate is semantically wrong and a maintenance trap: a future edit trusting the three-feature list would wrongly conclude clickhouse does not read these fields. Verified file:line: mod.rs:306-335 attributes/comment; main.rs:134-139 four-feature gate; main.rs:396-414 ClickHouse arm reads.
real.suggested_fix: Add `feature = "clickhouse-sink"` to the three `any(...)` lists at mod.rs:309-335 so the dead-code gate matches the four-feature set already used by `require_sink_url` (main.rs:134-139), and update the mod.rs:306 comment to reflect that all four non-noop sink arms (including clickhouse) read these fields.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at the cited lines. mod.rs:309-335 carries three identical #[cfg_attr(not(any(feature="http-sink",feature="mysql-sink",feature="postgres-sink")), allow(dead_code))] gates on sink_url, sink_timeout_secs, sink_max_batch_size — clickhouse-sink is omitted. The ClickHouse arm IS a non-noop arm that reads all three fields: require_sink_url(&config,"clickhouse") at main.rs:398 reads sink_url, config.sink_max_batch_size at main.rs:413, config.sink_timeout_secs at main.rs:414. The sibling helper require_sink_url's own #[cfg(any(...))] at main.rs:134-139 correctly lists all four features including clickhouse-sink, and the mod.rs:306 comment claims "only read by the non-noop sink arms." So the gate is semantically inconsistent with both its own comment and the sibling helper. The finding's harmlessness analysis is also correct: on a clickhouse-only build not(any(http,mysql,postgres)) is true so allow(dead_code) is applied, but since the fields are actually live there the allow is just a redundant no-op (an unnecessary allow does not warn), so -D warnings is not broken. This is a real doc/maintainability inconsistency and a maintenance trap, with no runtime/behavior impact.

real.title: DecodeError doc claims variants are 'ordered by the decode sequence' but the declared order contradicts the actual decode order
real.claim: error.rs:7-11 states the variant order is meaningful: 'Variants are ordered by the decode sequence: magic → version → header CRC → payload fields.' But the declared enum order is BadMagic, VersionMismatch, UnknownMessageType, UnknownDurability, HeaderCrcMismatch, PayloadCrcMismatch, TruncatedFrame, PayloadTooLarge (error.rs:15-51). The real decode sequence checks the header CRC (envelope.rs:178-185) BEFORE parsing the typed fields that yield UnknownMessageType/UnknownDurability (envelope.rs:187-190) — so HeaderCrcMismatch fires before those two at runtime yet is declared after them, directly contradicting the doc's own 'header CRC → payload fields' phrasing. Likewise in Envelope::decode, PayloadTooLarge (step 3, envelope.rs:277) fires before TruncatedFrame (step 4, envelope.rs:287) and PayloadCrcMismatch (step 5, envelope.rs:292), yet is declared last. A maintainer trusting the explicit 'ordered by the decode sequence' contract is misled. Distinct from F49/F51 (Fixed) and the 6 escalations.
real.suggested_fix: Reorder the variants to match the real decode order (BadMagic, VersionMismatch, HeaderCrcMismatch, UnknownMessageType, UnknownDurability, then PayloadTooLarge, TruncatedFrame, PayloadCrcMismatch), or soften the doc to drop the 'ordered by the decode sequence' claim.
real.verdict_reason:
Doc at crates/weir-core/src/error.rs:8 claims variants are ordered by the decode sequence (magic, version, header CRC, payload fields), but the enum declares UnknownMessageType (line 26) and UnknownDurability (line 28) before HeaderCrcMismatch (line 30), while the real decode (Header::decode, envelope.rs:178-190) checks header CRC at 178-185 before parsing those typed fields at 187-190. Header::decode's own doc (163-164) lists Header CRC as step 3 and Typed field parsing as step 4, confirming the runtime order and the contradiction with the declared order. Second claim also holds: PayloadTooLarge is declared last (line 46) but in Envelope::decode fires (line 277) before the payload-completeness TruncatedFrame (287) and PayloadCrcMismatch (295). Zero runtime impact: Nack mapping uses explicit match arms (server/src/socket/connection.rs:421+); the enum has no explicit discriminants and no as-cast discriminant use, so order is purely documentary. Genuine present doc inconsistency, distinct from F48/F49/F51 and the deferred escalations.</verdict_reason>
</invoke>

real.title: Payload's PartialEq-against-bytes impls are one-directional (payload == slice compiles, slice == payload does not)
real.claim: Payload defines `impl PartialEq<[u8]> for Payload` (payload.rs:99), `impl PartialEq<Vec<u8>> for Payload` (payload.rs:105), and `impl PartialEq<&[u8]> for Payload` (payload.rs:111) — all only in the `PartialEq<Other> for Payload` direction. There are no reverse impls, so `payload == some_slice` compiles but `some_slice == payload` does not. The Rust API guidelines (C-COMMON-TRAITS) treat PartialEq as conceptually symmetric and std follows this for analogous cross-type comparisons (e.g. both `String: PartialEq<str>` and `str: PartialEq<String>`). F51 (Fixed, commit b446533) added round-trip/Hash tests but only for the forward direction (tests at payload.rs:165-179), freezing the asymmetry into the 1.0 surface. Adding the reverse impls after 1.0 is non-breaking, so low urgency, but worth a deliberate call before freeze rather than by omission. Genuinely new (F51 was a test-coverage fix, not this asymmetry).
real.suggested_fix: Either add the three reverse `impl PartialEq<Payload> for X` impls now for symmetry, or document that comparison is Payload-on-the-left only.
real.verdict_reason:
Verified at payload.rs lines 99, 105, 111: all three cross-type PartialEq impls are written as impl PartialEq for Payload in the forward direction only (PartialEq of u8 slice, PartialEq of Vec of u8, PartialEq of reference to u8 slice). Grep confirms no reverse impl exists in the file, and the derive at line 16 yields only Payload versus Payload. The F51 tests at lines 165-179 assert only the forward direction (payload on the left), never slice on the left. So payload equals slice compiles but slice equals payload does not, exactly as the finding states. The std analogy is correct: std provides both String PartialEq str and str PartialEq String. Not a duplicate of F51, which was purely a test-coverage finding (impls untested) and never raised the directional asymmetry. Not freeze-gated: adding the three reverse impls post-1.0 is a non-breaking additive change permitted by the orphan rule (the RHS Payload type is local). Severity low is correct: pure API ergonomics, no runtime or invariant impact.</verdict_reason>
</invoke>

real.title: F05 left the canonical drain state-machine diagram(s) stale: cap-clears no longer goes to Draining/"retry from start"
real.claim: The module-level state-machine ASCII diagram still ends with `BlockedDeadLetterFull ──(cap clears)──▶ Draining (same segment, retry from start)` (drain/mod.rs:17). The F05 fix (commit bb7650e, in-scope post-65a39fb) rewrote exactly this transition: when the dead-letter cap clears with headroom, the code now returns `DrainState::RetryingTransient { segment, retries_left: config.max_retries, next_delay: Duration::ZERO, processed }` (mod.rs:452-457) and deliberately RESUMES at `skip = processed` (mod.rs:438-445, process_segment skip handling at mod.rs:651-656) rather than re-reading from record 0. So the diagram now misstates both the target state (says Draining; is RetryingTransient) and the resume semantics (says "retry from start"; actually resumes past already-processed sub-batches — the whole point of F05). The author updated the inline body comments (mod.rs:439-445) and the resume test but missed this top-of-file diagram, leaving it the only place in the file still asserting the pre-F05 "retry from start" behaviour a reader would trust. The same drift exists in the second diagram in docs/architecture.md:145 (`bytes < cap → unblock, push segment to front of pending queue, Draining` — the code no longer push_front's onto pending nor returns Draining); architecture.md:147 (`channel disconnect + bytes < cap → confirm segment, exit`) is likewise inaccurate but predates 65a39fb so is not new.
real.suggested_fix: Update the mod.rs:17 diagram's last transition to e.g. `BlockedDeadLetterFull ──(cap clears)──▶ RetryingTransient (same segment, RESUMING past already-processed sub-batches)`, matching the inline comments at mod.rs:439-445. Apply the same correction to docs/architecture.md:145 (and fix the long-standing line 147 while editing the block).
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at the cited locations. drain/mod.rs:17 diagram still reads `BlockedDeadLetterFull ──(cap clears)──▶ Draining (same segment, retry from start)`, but the F05 fix (bb7650e, in-scope post-65a39fb) at mod.rs:452-457 returns `DrainState::RetryingTransient { segment, retries_left, next_delay: Duration::ZERO, processed }` on cap-clear, and process_segment resumes past the already-processed prefix via the skip check at mod.rs:651-656 (`if read_index <= skip { continue; }`). So the diagram misstates both the target state (Draining vs RetryingTransient) and resume semantics (from start vs resume past processed). `git show bb7650e -- drain/mod.rs` confirms the commit added the corrected inline comments (mod.rs:439-445) but never touched the top diagram, leaving it the lone stale spot in the file. docs/architecture.md:145 (`push segment to front of pending queue, Draining`) is likewise stale — the code no longer push_fronts onto pending nor returns Draining. architecture.md:147 is also inaccurate but predates 65a39fb (correctly flagged as not-new by the finder).

real.title: F05 post-block retry seeds next_delay=ZERO, defeating exponential backoff into a no-backoff busy-retry loop on subsequent transient sink errors
real.claim: When a dead-letter-blocked segment gets headroom, the drain now routes the retry through `DrainState::RetryingTransient { next_delay: Duration::ZERO, retries_left: config.max_retries, .. }` (mod.rs:452-457, introduced by F05/bb7650e which replaced the prior `pending.push_front(segment); Draining` path). ZERO is intended only as "retry immediately" (comment mod.rs:443-445). But if that resumed attempt then hits a TRANSIENT sink error (not blocked), the RetryingTransient arm recomputes the next delay as `next_retry_delay(next_delay * 2, retry_after)` = `next_retry_delay(Duration::ZERO * 2, None)` (mod.rs:381). next_retry_delay (mod.rs:559-562) returns `hint.unwrap_or(default).min(MAX)` = ZERO when the default is ZERO and there is no Retry-After hint, so `std::thread::sleep(next_delay)` at mod.rs:352 sleeps 0 again. The segment therefore spin-retries the sink with NO backoff for the whole retries_left budget (default MAX_RETRIES=3). The first-failure path (transition_from_draining, mod.rs:529-534) correctly seeds `next_delay: next_retry_delay(config.base_retry_delay, ..)`, so this is a backoff regression specific to the post-block recovery path — exactly when the sink is under pressure and backoff matters most. A sink-supplied Retry-After hint still overrides ZERO, so only the no-hint transient case spins. Bounded by max_retries, hence low severity, but unintended (the comment documents only the immediate first retry, not a no-backoff loop).
real.suggested_fix: Seed the post-headroom RetryingTransient with `next_delay: config.base_retry_delay` (matching the Draining path) so the immediate retry still fires (delay can be made 0 only for that first attempt) but subsequent transient failures back off exponentially. Alternatively, have next_retry_delay treat a ZERO default as base_retry_delay.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at the cited locations. The post-block headroom path (drain/mod.rs:452-457) seeds DrainState::RetryingTransient { next_delay: Duration::ZERO, retries_left: config.max_retries }. The RetryingTransient arm sleeps next_delay (line 352) then, on a subsequent transient failure with no Retry-After hint, recomputes via next_retry_delay(next_delay * 2, retry_after) (line 381). Duration::ZERO * 2 == Duration::ZERO, and next_retry_delay (lines 559-562) returns hint.unwrap_or(default).min(MAX) = ZERO when default is ZERO and hint is None. So sleep(ZERO) repeats for the full remaining budget (max_retries=3), each iteration re-issuing sink.commit (line 744) with no pacing — a no-backoff busy-retry against a fast-failing transient sink. This contrasts correctly with the first-failure path transition_from_draining (line 532), which seeds next_delay: next_retry_delay(config.base_retry_delay=100ms, ..) and doubles properly. The arithmetic and the regression characterization in the finding are accurate. A Retry-After hint still overrides ZERO, so only the no-hint transient case spins, and it is bounded by max_retries — consistent with low severity.

real.title: F05 completeness assert debug_assert_eq!(durable_through, read_index) runs before the read_failed check and is violated by a mid-prefix read error on a resumed segment
real.claim: The F05 refactor (bb7650e) added `debug_assert_eq!(durable_through, read_index);` at drain/mod.rs:698, which runs UNCONDITIONALLY before the `if read_failed` quarantine branch at line 700. The asserted invariant (all readable records durably processed) holds for the read-failed path in general because the trailing non-empty batch is still committed at mod.rs:679-695 — EXCEPT when the read error lands inside the already-skipped prefix of a resumed segment. Reachable case: process_segment is called with `skip = processed > 0` (mod.rs:362-369 / 452-457). On the re-read the reader returns Err while read_index is still <= skip (records 1..skip are `continue`d at mod.rs:654-656 and never entered a batch). The error arm (mod.rs:640-649) sets read_failed=true and breaks WITHOUT incrementing read_index for the failed record. At the assert point durable_through == skip (its initial value, never advanced — no batch committed) but read_index < skip → the assert fails. SegmentReader::next (wab/mod.rs:775-820) genuinely returns Some(Err(_)) on a read_exact failure or a CRC mismatch mid-stream, and a previously-clean prefix record can fail to re-read on a transient I/O fault or post-seal bit-rot (the comment at mod.rs:641 explicitly contemplates "a transient read error"), so the scenario is reachable, if uncommon. In debug/test builds this panics process_segment; run_drain_supervised (mod.rs ~161, MAX_DRAIN_RESPAWNS=10) catches it and respawns, dropping the in-flight segment from the channel for this run (still durable on disk, replayed on restart). In release builds the debug_assert is compiled out and the segment correctly falls through to the read_failed quarantine branch — no incorrect behaviour. Net: a newly-introduced assertion is placed on a path where the invariant is false by construction, crashing debug/test builds on a legitimate mid-prefix read fault during a resume.
real.suggested_fix: Guard the assert with the non-failed path, e.g. `if !read_failed { debug_assert_eq!(durable_through, read_index); }`, or move it just before the final `ProcessResult::Confirmed { record_count: read_index }` return (after the read_failed early-return), so the completeness check only runs where the invariant actually holds.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at drain/mod.rs. The assert `debug_assert_eq!(durable_through, read_index)` (line 698) was added by the F05 fix bb7650e and runs unconditionally before the `if read_failed` quarantine branch (line 700). Trace of the cited scenario with skip=N>0: read_index init 0 (632), durable_through init skip (633). For each yielded prefix record, line 651 does `read_index += 1` then line 654 `if read_index <= skip { continue; }`, so nothing enters `batch`. If a prefix record (position j <= skip) yields Err, the error arm (640-649) sets read_failed=true and breaks WITHOUT incrementing read_index, leaving read_index = j-1 < skip. `batch` is empty so the trailing commit (679) is skipped and durable_through stays at skip. Thus durable_through(skip) != read_index(j-1) and the debug_assert panics. SegmentReader::next genuinely yields Some(Err(_)) mid-stream on read_exact failure (wab/mod.rs:777,801,808) or CRC mismatch (814), so a transient I/O fault or post-seal bit-rot on a previously-clean prefix record is reachable. Impact is correctly bounded: debug/test builds panic -> run_drain_supervised (161-194, MAX_DRAIN_RESPAWNS=10) catches and respawns, segment deferred to restart (still durable, crown invariant intact); release builds compile the assert out and fall through correctly to the read_failed quarantine branch (700-718). Boundary is exact: error at position skip+1 makes read_index==skip and the assert passes, so the bug is strictly errors at positions <= skip, matching the finding's scoping. Severity low is well-calibrated (debug/test-only spurious panic on a legitimate fault path; no production-correctness or crown-invariant impact). Suggested guard `if !read_failed { ... }` is valid.

real.title: F05 retry/unblock re-reads, re-allocates and re-CRCs every already-processed prefix record before skipping it
real.claim: The F05 sub-batch-resume path (process_segment, drain/mod.rs:632-657) resumes a retried or post-block segment by opening a fresh SegmentReader and iterating from record 0, skipping the already-durable prefix with `read_index += 1; if read_index <= skip { continue; }` (mod.rs:651-656). The skip is applied only AFTER SegmentReader::next (wab/mod.rs:766-823) has done the full per-record work: read the 4-byte length and 4-byte CRC, heap-allocate `vec![0u8; payload_len]` (wab/mod.rs:805), read_exact the payload, and compute `crc32fast::hash(&payload_buf)` (wab/mod.rs:811) — all then discarded by the continue. So every transient retry (up to max_retries per attempt) and every BlockedDeadLetterFull→RetryingTransient unblock re-pays a full buffered read + heap allocation + CRC32 over the entire already-processed prefix; under a flapping dead-letter cap the same prefix is re-scanned on each unblock. Correctness is unaffected (the prefix is correctly skipped) but this is avoidable redundant I/O, heap churn and CRC work on the recovery/retry path, in newly-written code (post-65a39fb).
real.suggested_fix: Add a SegmentReader fast-forward that reads only the 8-byte length+CRC framing and advances the BufReader by payload_len without allocating or CRC'ing skipped records, or persist the byte offset of the resume point so the reader can seek directly to it instead of re-walking from record 0.
real.verdict_reason:
Confirmed at every cited line. process_segment (drain/mod.rs:595-603) opens a fresh SegmentReader that seeks only past the segment header, then iterates from record 0; the skip is applied at lines 651-656 (read_index += 1; if read_index <= skip { continue; }) only AFTER SegmentReader::next (wab/mod.rs:766-823) has read len+CRC, heap-allocated vec![0u8; payload_len] (line 805), read_exact'd the payload (806), and computed crc32fast::hash (811). RetryingTransient (362-369) re-invokes process_segment with skip=processed, and the BlockedDeadLetterFull unblock routes through RetryingTransient carrying processed (440-456), so each transient retry and each cap-unblock re-pays a full buffered read + heap allocation + CRC over the entire already-durable prefix. Correctness is intact (durable_through starts at skip; prefix correctly skipped); the cost is redundant cold-path I/O/heap/CRC only. New code post-65a39fb (the F05 resume work).</verdict_reason>
</invoke>

real.title: weir_wab_bytes_on_disk double-counts dead-letter and quarantine bytes (no reserved-subdir skip)
real.claim: compute_wab_bytes_on_disk (main.rs:58-80, wired to the wab_bytes_on_disk gauge at main.rs:484-488 every 5s) walks every subdirectory of wab_dir and sums any file whose name ends in `.wab` or `.wab.sealed` (line 74). But wab_dir contains two reserved sibling subdirectories that hold non-live-shard data: `dead_letter/` (files named dl_NNNNNNNN.wab.sealed — dead_letter.rs:6,98,104) and `quarantine/` (segments renamed to `<shard>__<orig>.wab.sealed` — recovery.rs:358-377). Recovery deliberately skips BOTH by name (recovery.rs:47: `if name == "quarantine" || name == "dead_letter" { continue; }`), but this metric function has no such guard — it descends into them unconditionally. Result: (1) every dead-letter byte is counted twice, once in weir_dead_letter_bytes_on_disk (the drain's own gauge — metrics/mod.rs:451, updated at drain/mod.rs:428/829/900) and again in weir_wab_bytes_on_disk; and (2) parked quarantine bytes are attributed to the live WAB. The gauge is documented as 'Current total bytes used by WAB segment files on disk' (metrics/mod.rs:382), so an operator alerting on WAB backlog growth gets readings inflated by dead-letter/quarantine backlog — conflating the exact conditions the two gauges exist to separate. (Distinct from the already-fixed F16, which was recovery re-sealing dead_letter as a shard dir; this is the metric-path analogue at a different file:line.)
real.suggested_fix: In compute_wab_bytes_on_disk, mirror the recovery.rs:47 guard: skip subdirectories named `dead_letter` and `quarantine` before descending, so the gauge reflects only live shard segments.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at all cited locations. compute_wab_bytes_on_disk (main.rs:58-80) descends into every is_dir() subdirectory of wab_dir and sums files ending in .wab/.wab.sealed (line 74) with NO reserved-subdir guard. Recovery explicitly skips both reserved siblings (recovery.rs:47: `if name == "quarantine" || name == "dead_letter" { continue; }`). dead_letter/ lives under wab_dir (dead_letter.rs:52) holding dl_NNNNNNNN.wab sealed to .wab.sealed (lines 98,104); quarantine/ lives under wab_dir (recovery.rs:359) holding <shard>__<orig>.wab.sealed files (line 369). Both suffixes match the metric filter, so dead-letter bytes are double-counted against the dedicated weir_dead_letter_bytes_on_disk gauge (metrics/mod.rs:451) and parked quarantine bytes are misattributed to live WAB, contradicting the gauge's documented meaning (metrics/mod.rs:382). Gauge is wired every 5s at main.rs:483-488. Citation paths omit the wab/ and drain/ prefixes but every line number and code claim is exact.

real.title: Metrics HTTP accept loop exits permanently on any transient accept() error
real.claim: serve()'s accept loop treats EVERY accept() error as fatal: `let Ok((stream, _peer)) = listener.accept().await else { return; };` (server.rs:55-57). tokio's TcpListener::accept surfaces transient, recoverable errors — ECONNABORTED (peer reset between SYN and accept) and EMFILE/ENFILE/ENOBUFS (fd/buffer exhaustion) — directly to the caller without retrying. A single such error permanently kills the metrics endpoint for the rest of the daemon's lifetime, with no log, no metric, and no recovery until restart — most likely to fire precisely under fd pressure, exactly when operators most need metrics. The doc at server.rs:37-38 claims the task exits only on an 'unrecoverable accept error', but the code makes no recoverable/unrecoverable distinction. The data-path accept loop already recognises this class and backs off/retries instead of dying (is_accept_resource_exhaustion in socket/mod.rs:207). This is a separate mechanism from the already-fixed F20 (which added per-handler SCRAPE_IO_TIMEOUT for slowloris/parked handlers); F20 did not touch the accept loop's error handling.
real.suggested_fix: On accept() Err, log and `continue` (optionally with a brief backoff on resource-exhaustion errors, reusing is_accept_resource_exhaustion from socket/mod.rs) instead of returning, so a transient EMFILE/ECONNABORTED doesn't permanently take down metrics exposition.
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at crates/weir-server/src/metrics/server.rs:55-57: `let Ok((stream, _peer)) = listener.accept().await else { return; };` returns from serve() on ANY accept() Err, permanently ending the metrics task with no log/metric/retry. The doc at server.rs:37-38 claims exit only on "unrecoverable accept error" but the code makes no recoverable/unrecoverable distinction. tokio's TcpListener::accept does surface transient errors (ECONNABORTED, EMFILE/ENFILE/ENOBUFS) to the caller without retrying, so a single fd-pressure error kills metrics exposition until restart — precisely when operators need it. The asymmetry is concrete: the data-path accept loop (socket/mod.rs:205-216) handles the identical error class by logging, classifying via is_accept_resource_exhaustion (mod.rs:308-313 covers EMFILE/ENFILE/ENOBUFS/ENOMEM), backing off, and continuing the loop — never returning.

real.title: ClientTlsConfig implements no Debug despite holding only Debug-able, non-secret fields
real.claim: `ClientTlsConfig<'a>` (tls.rs:14-25) is a public struct whose every field is Debug-able and non-secret (`&Path` x3, `&str`, `Option<Durability>`), yet it derives nothing — no Debug, no Clone. The Rust API guideline C-DEBUG ('all public types implement Debug') flags exactly this, and sibling public config-like types in the workspace do derive Debug (e.g. CommitResult/SinkHealth in weir-sink-sdk/src/lib.rs). Adding the derive is non-breaking later but easy to forget once the surface is locked, and a caller hits it the moment they try to log or assert on a TLS config. No fixed/deferred item covers this type.
real.suggested_fix: Add `#[derive(Debug, Clone)]` to ClientTlsConfig — the fields are paths/strings and leak no key material (only file paths).
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at crates/weir-client/src/tls.rs:14-25: ClientTlsConfig<'a> is a public struct with NO #[derive(...)] line and no manual Debug impl. All five fields are Debug-able and non-secret: client_cert/client_key/ca_cert are &Path (file paths, not key material), server_name is &str, default_durability is Option<Durability> where Durability derives Debug (durability.rs:7). The crate otherwise follows a Debug convention — ClientError derives Debug (unix.rs:13) and WeirClient derives Debug (unix.rs:81 region) — and sink-sdk siblings CommitResult/SinkHealth derive Debug (lib.rs:106,115). So this is the lone public client type missing Debug, exactly the C-DEBUG gap claimed. The claim's facts all hold right now.

real.title: set_read_timeout doc intra-doc link labels `read_response` but targets the wrong (private) item
real.claim: The set_read_timeout doc (unix.rs:241-242) reads 'every method blocks in [`read_response`][Self::push] waiting for the daemon's Ack/Nack'. The link text is `read_response` but the target is `Self::push`; read_response (unix.rs:154) is a private fn, not linkable, so rustdoc renders the text 'read_response' as a link to `push` — a confusing mismatch in the most safety-relevant doc on the client (the availability/retry guidance, which also underpins the desync hazard above). No `deny(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)` is set, so it won't fail CI, but it ships as misleading rendered docs. The same prose ('block a producer forever in `read_response`') is echoed at tls.rs:78-79.
real.suggested_fix: Drop the broken link and say 'blocks in the response-read path waiting for the daemon's Ack/Nack', or link to the actual public method (push/health_check).
real.verdict_reason: Confirmed at unix.rs:242: the doc comment contains [`read_response`][Self::push] — link text "read_response" but target Self::push. read_response (unix.rs:154) is a private fn (no pub), so it cannot be an intra-doc-link target; push (unix.rs:101) is public and the link resolves, so rustdoc renders the visible label "read_response" as a working link that jumps to push — a real label/target mismatch. No #![deny(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)] exists in the crate (only #![deny(missing_docs)]), and since the target resolves it would not trip that lint anyway, so CI is unaffected; it ships as misleading rendered docs only. One caveat: the tls.rs:78-79 echo uses plain backticks (inline code read_response), not the [text][target] link syntax, so it is not a broken link — only imprecise prose. The core unix.rs:242 mismatch stands.

real.title: Wire enum TryFrom error structs (UnknownMessageType/UnknownDurability/UnknownNackReason) are not re-exported at the crate root, unlike the enums they belong to
real.claim: lib.rs:29-34 re-exports the three frozen wire enums flat at the crate root (`pub use durability::Durability; pub use envelope::{...MessageType}; pub use nack::NackReason;`) and the crate doc (lib.rs:7-16, 'What's here') presents those flat re-exports as THE public API. But each enum's public `TryFrom<u8>` impl uses a separate, public error struct that is NOT re-exported: `UnknownMessageType` (envelope.rs:52, used as the `type Error` at envelope.rs:63), `UnknownDurability` (durability.rs:20, error at durability.rs:31), `UnknownNackReason` (nack.rs:35, error at nack.rs:57). An external caller who writes `weir_core::Durability::try_from(byte)` and wants to name/match/store the resulting error must reach into the undocumented sub-module path `weir_core::durability::UnknownDurability`. These structs are part of the frozen 1.0 surface (they appear in public `TryFrom` signatures), so this is a discoverability/consistency gap that becomes a breaking move-or-rename after 1.0. Confirmed not in the Fixed table nor the 6 escalations.
real.suggested_fix: Re-export the three error structs from the crate root next to their enums (e.g. `pub use durability::{Durability, UnknownDurability};`), or explicitly document the sub-module path. Free now, a breaking change after 1.0.
real.verdict_reason: All factual claims hold at the cited locations. The three error structs are `pub` and used as the `type Error` in public `TryFrom<u8>` impls: `UnknownMessageType` (envelope.rs:52, error at :63), `UnknownDurability` (durability.rs:20, error at :31), `UnknownNackReason` (nack.rs:35, error at :57). lib.rs:29-34 re-exports only the three enums (Durability, MessageType, NackReason) flat at the crate root, not these error structs, so an external caller invoking e.g. `weir_core::Durability::try_from(byte)` who wants to name/match the error must reach into `weir_core::durability::UnknownDurability`. The crate doc (lib.rs:7-16) presents the flat re-exports as the public API but omits these. Not a duplicate of F25 (which concerns Nack-reason mapping + connection close, a different semantic issue) and not in the Fixed table or the 6 escalations. However, two points cut the severity to the floor: (1) the canonical decode path `Header::decode` (envelope.rs:165-201) immediately maps these structs into re-exported `DecodeError` variants (`.map_err(|e| DecodeError::UnknownMessageType(e.0))`, envelope.rs:188/190), and `DecodeError` (lib.rs:31) carries the raw byte directly — so the standalone structs only surface if a caller bypasses decode and calls `TryFrom` directly, an uncommon path. (2) The finding's "breaking after 1.0" urgency is overstated: adding a crate-root re-export later is purely additive and non-breaking; only renaming/relocating the struct in its module would break, which is orthogonal to whether the re-export exists.

real.title: Envelope::decode silently ignores trailing bytes past frame_len — undocumented and untested in the wire reference crate
real.claim: Envelope::decode treats `buf.len() < frame_len` as TruncatedFrame (envelope.rs:287) but accepts any `buf.len() >= frame_len`, decoding the first frame and silently discarding everything after byte `frame_len` (= HEADER_LEN + payload_len + 4). This crate is the executable reference for the v1 wire format (lib.rs:18-19), yet the decode doc (envelope.rs:255-265) lists five validation steps and never states what happens to trailing bytes, and no test exercises an over-long buffer. Harmless for weir's own socket/client paths (they read exactly frame_len), but an external implementer comparing against this reference has no spec or test pinning whether 'extra bytes after a complete frame' is rejected or ignored — a latent interop ambiguity to settle at the 1.0 wire freeze. Not covered by the Fixed table or the wire-freeze escalations (F25/F50/F52 concern Nack mapping, payload_len arg, and reserved flags respectively).
real.suggested_fix: Document the contract on Envelope::decode ('bytes beyond the declared frame length are ignored; the caller owns framing') and add a test asserting a valid frame with appended garbage decodes to the same Envelope — or, if exact-length is intended, return an error for trailing data. Lock the semantics before 1.0.
real.verdict_reason: All load-bearing claims hold at the cited locations. envelope.rs:287 checks `buf.len() < frame_len` and accepts any `buf.len() >= frame_len`, then slices only `buf[HEADER_LEN..HEADER_LEN+payload_len]` and `[..frame_len]` (lines 291-293) — trailing bytes past frame_len are silently ignored. The decode doc comment (envelope.rs:255-265) enumerates exactly five validation steps and never mentions trailing-byte handling. lib.rs:18-19 explicitly designates this crate as the wire format's executable reference. No test in envelope.rs, tests/reference_frames.rs, or tests/proptest_envelope.rs exercises an over-long buffer: the proptest suite covers only short buffers (short_buffer_rejected_as_truncated) and truncated payloads (truncated_payload_rejected), never a complete frame with appended bytes. The finding correctly concedes this is harmless for weir's own paths — confirmed at socket/connection.rs:109-228, which reads HEADER_LEN then exactly payload_len then 4 CRC bytes via read_exact (length-prefixed framing), so the server never hands a multi-frame buffer to Envelope::decode. The wire doc (lines 128-134) confirms pipelining happens at the kernel level with serial server-side framing, not via decode on a concatenated buffer. Not a duplicate: prior findings F25/F50/F52 concern Nack mapping, payload_len arg, and reserved flags respectively; the nearest (F52, flags preserve-and-ignore) is a distinct concern, and no prior FINDINGS entry addresses trailing bytes. This is a genuine documentation/test gap in the reference crate, accurately self-rated.

real.title: client_cert_cn parses the full X.509 client certificate (parse + String alloc) on every successful mTLS handshake regardless of log level, purely to populate a debug! field
real.claim: On every successful mTLS handshake the accept task unconditionally binds `let cn = client_cert_cn(&tls_stream);` (tcp.rs:225, verified) before the only consumer, `debug!(client_cn = ?cn, ...)` on the very next line (tcp.rs:226). client_cert_cn runs x509_parser::parse_x509_certificate over the peer-cert DER and allocates a String (tcp.rs:336-346, verified). Because cn is bound eagerly, the parse + allocation happen even at the production-default `info` level where the debug event is never emitted — pure wasted work plus one allocation per connection on the accept hot path, and a small attacker-influenced parse cost (a peer that completes the handshake controls the cert bytes fed to the parser). The function's own doc states it is 'purely diagnostic'.
real.suggested_fix: Gate the parse behind the active level, e.g. compute cn only inside `if tracing::enabled!(tracing::Level::DEBUG)`, or pass it as a lazily-evaluated tracing field (closure/valuable) so the parse runs only when the debug event is actually recorded.
real.verdict_reason: All cited facts verified. tcp.rs:225 binds `let cn = client_cert_cn(&tls_stream);` eagerly, before its sole consumer `debug!(%peer_addr, client_cn = ?cn, "TLS handshake ok")` at line 226. The `?cn` field is evaluated when constructing the call, so the parse runs even at `info` level where the event is dropped. client_cert_cn (lines 336-346) does call x509_parser::parse_x509_certificate over the peer DER and allocates a String via .to_string(). So the claim genuinely holds: one DER parse + one String alloc per successful mTLS handshake regardless of log level. Caveats reducing impact: (a) this is per successful handshake, not per request/record, and a TLS handshake already costs far more (asymmetric crypto + CA path validation) than a single DER parse, so the relative overhead is negligible; (b) the "attacker-influenced parse" angle is weak because WebPki CA-path validation has already proven the cert is CA-signed and well-formed before this runs.

refuted.title: Graceful-shutdown drain budget (shutdown_timeout_secs) is never validated against the hardcoded 30s ACK_TIMEOUT, so the documented drain precondition is silently unmet — in-flight handlers can be abort_all'd before their ack/nack completes
refuted.claim: Both drain loops document a hard precondition in their own comments: 'The timeout should be >= ACK_TIMEOUT + buffer so legitimate in-flight work completes before abort_all is reached' (mod.rs:231-233, tcp.rs:267-268). Nothing enforces it. ACK_TIMEOUT is a hardcoded 30s (connection.rs:35, verified). shutdown_timeout_secs defaults to exactly 30 and is validated ONLY to be != 0 (config/mod.rs:519-525, verified — the only check is `== 0 -> error`). So (a) the DEFAULT config leaves ZERO buffer: a handler still awaiting the ack oneshot in handle_push (capped by ACK_TIMEOUT = 30s) competes with drain_join_set's identical 30s budget, a dead heat in which join_set.abort_all() (mod.rs:249 / tcp.rs:283) can fire at or before the nack is written; and (b) an operator can legally set shutdown_timeout_secs = 1, guaranteeing abort_all of any handler waiting on a slow flusher. The crown invariant is NOT violated (the producer gets no response and retries — no false ack), but the documented graceful-drain contract is silently unmet, and connections_aborted_at_shutdown (mod.rs:246-248 / tcp.rs:280-282) spikes with no startup diagnostic pointing at a mis-sized timeout.
refuted.suggested_fix: At minimum emit a startup warning when shutdown_timeout_secs < ACK_TIMEOUT + a small buffer, so the unmet precondition is visible. Stronger: validate shutdown_timeout_secs >= ACK_TIMEOUT in config (warn-or-reject), or derive one from the other instead of letting two independent 30s constants race.
refuted.verdict_reason: All cited facts verified at HEAD. ACK_TIMEOUT is a hardcoded 30s (connection.rs:35). The handle_push ack wait is capped by exactly that timeout (connection.rs:362, ack_timeout==ACK_TIMEOUT). shutdown_timeout_secs defaults to 30 and is validated ONLY against ==0 (config/mod.rs:519-525) — no relation to ACK_TIMEOUT is enforced. Both drain loops document the precondition "timeout should be >= ACK_TIMEOUT + buffer" (mod.rs:231-233, tcp.rs:267-268) yet nothing checks it, and abort_all fires at mod.rs:249 / tcp.rs:283 with no startup diagnostic — only the runtime counter connections_aborted_at_shutdown (metrics/mod.rs:182-187). So the default leaves zero buffer and an operator can set shutdown_timeout_secs=1, and the documented graceful-drain contract is genuinely unenforced/unwarned. Crown invariant is NOT violated (no false ack; at-least-once + abort_all backstop), as the finding itself states. The proposed fix is low-risk and already has a sibling precedent: config/mod.rs:403-409 pushes a post-validation advisory warning for a too-small dead_letter_max_bytes, structurally identical to the suggested shutdown_timeout warning.
